titleStmt.title: Letter from Robert Brown to Sir Matthew Nathan, 15 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Robert Brown

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Robert Brown to Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939). In the letter Brown refers to the fraud case of Henry McCambley, for which he was asked by the Leith police to attend at witness. Brown goes on to state that his expenses were payed out of his pocket and that he lost wages by attending the trail. For this he requests compensation stating that it was over 6 months since he lost his earnings.Nathan was the under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan offered his resignation following the outbreak of the Easter Rising. Robert Brown was the assistant manager at the National Margarine Company Ltd. Before the war, a middle-class British household tended to put butter on the table, and reserve margarine for cooking purposes. But because the rations for the margarines were more generous than for butter, margarine worked its way out of the kitchen and into the dining room.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5613/6825

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
The National Margarine Company
Limited Inspector General, R.26
For report, please,
as to A.
17 â 4 â 16 10, Quality Street, Leith 15th. April 1916 To The Under Secretary, Dublin Castle, DUBLIN. Dear Sir, Re MeCambley alleged fraud. It was requested by the Belfast Police Officials (the request
being communicated through the chief Constable Leith)to proceed
to Belfast to give information regarding the alleged fraud. Complying with the request I proceeded to Belfast on Oct.8th retur
âning home on Oct.10th.1915.The sum actually paid out of my pock
âet for expenses incurred amounted to Â£2/13/4 and I suffered consid
âerable loss through being absent from my work. I have received
through the Leith Police the sum of Â£2/13/4for travelling expen
âses but my claim for the sum of Â£1/1/0 for loss of time and em
âployment is evidently not forthcoming,As it is over six months
since I sustained the loss of earnings I think it is now time that
the sum was paid over to me.I shall thank you to give instruct
âions for payment to be made without further delay. Yours truly, for The National Margarine
Roc Brown Assistant Manager
